subsections.title: Self Introduction & Communication
subsections.keyRule: For 12+ years: Lead with impact, not timeline. Structure: Domain expertise → Key system you designed → One architecture decision you made → Business result. Keep it 2-3 minutes. Every sentence must answer 'So what?' Interviewers at this level evaluate whether you think like an architect, not just a developer.

terms.what: A structured 2-3 minute professional pitch that communicates your technical depth, domain expertise, architecture decisions you made, and business impact — NOT a timeline of job titles.
terms.why: At 12+ years, interviewers expect you to articulate systems you designed, tradeoffs you made, and results you drove. They are evaluating technical leadership, communication, and seniority of thinking — not just skill checklist.
terms.how: Structure in 5 parts: 1) Role + Domain: 'I am a senior Java engineer/architect with 12+ years, currently working in healthcare interoperability.' 2) Flagship System: 'I have been building eCR-Now, a FHIR-based electronic case reporting middleware deployed on GKE that connects hospital EHR systems to the CDC for automated disease surveillance.' 3) One Key Architecture Decision: 'I designed the async patient data flow — when a patient is admitted, IMS sends a launchPatient POST through our API gateway, eCR-Now queries FHIR for reportable conditions, generates the eCR report, sends to CDC via Direct messaging protocol, then posts the CDC response back to the EHR as a FHIR documentReference.' 4) Scale/Impact: 'The system handles admissions for multiple hospital networks, with 99.9% CDC reporting compliance.' 5) Bridge to Conversation: 'Happy to go deeper on the FHIR integration, GKE deployment, or failure handling — what is most relevant for your team?'
terms.where: Every interview — phone screen, technical round, architecture discussion, panel interview, CTO/VP level meeting. This sets the tone for everything that follows.
terms.when: Always lead with this. It frames you as someone who thinks in systems. The last line invites them to steer toward their interests, giving you control of the conversation.
terms.issue: Most 12+ year developers give a resume-reading intro: 'I worked at X, then Y, then Z, I know Spring Boot and Kafka.' This sounds junior regardless of experience — it lists tools without context of what was built or why.
terms.fix: Prepare 3 versions: 60-second elevator pitch, 2-minute technical intro, 5-minute deep-dive. Practice each until natural, not scripted. Always anchor to one real system you are most proud of and describe it architecturally.

star.s: Interviewer opened: 'Tell me about yourself.' Architecture-level role at a health-tech company.
star.t: Position myself as a senior architect who thinks in systems and business outcomes, not just as a developer who knows frameworks.
star.a: Led with domain (healthcare interoperability), named the system (eCR-Now on GKE), described one architectural flow end-to-end (patient admission → FHIR query → CDC report → EHR response), stated the business result (99.9% CDC reporting compliance), then invited them to steer: 'Happy to go deeper on FHIR, GKE, or our failure handling approach — what matters most for your team?'
star.r: Interviewer said: 'Let us talk about the FHIR integration.' I had already demonstrated depth before the first technical question was asked.

terms.what: Explaining your project at architecture level means covering: business problem → system components → data flow → technology choices with rationale → scalability/failure handling → operational observability. Not listing technologies.
terms.why: Senior engineers are expected to communicate systems to non-technical stakeholders, guide junior developers, and make and defend technology decisions. Architecture-level communication is the single biggest differentiator at 12+ years versus 5 years.
terms.how: Six-part structure: 1) Business Problem: 'When a hospital admits a patient with a reportable condition (flu, COVID, etc.), they are legally required to notify the CDC within 24 hours. This was done manually — we automated it.' 2) High-Level Architecture: 'eCR-Now is a middleware service on GKE that sits between the hospital EHR (Evident) and the CDC. It integrates via FHIR R4 standard.' 3) Data Flow: 'Patient admitted in IMS → IMS uses cpXML to get FHIR IDs → Sends launchPatient POST to our API Gateway (Linus) → Gateway routes to eCR-Now on GKE → eCR-Now queries patient FHIR resources (Condition, Observation, MedicationRequest) → Evaluates reportability triggers → Generates eCR report → Sends to CDC via Direct messaging (secure SMTP) → Polls IMAP for CDC response → Stores response in eCR-Now DB → POSTs documentReference back to Evident EHR.' 4) Key Technology Decisions: 'FHIR because it is the national interoperability standard — every modern EHR supports it. GKE for pod autoscaling — burst admission load during morning rounds. Direct protocol because CDC mandates it for secure health messaging.' 5) Failure Handling: 'Circuit breakers on every FHIR call. Retry with exponential backoff for CDC submissions. Dead-letter queue for failed reports. Liveness probes restart stuck pods automatically.' 6) Observability: 'Prometheus metrics, Grafana dashboards, distributed tracing with correlation IDs across the full patient flow.'
terms.where: Architecture interviews, technical design reviews, CTO presentations, onboarding new team members, stakeholder demos.
terms.when: After self-intro. Prepare a 5-minute version and a 10-minute deep-dive. Draw the flow on whiteboard when available — visual always wins over verbal alone.
terms.issue: Engineers describe projects as 'We built REST APIs with Spring Boot connected to MySQL.' No context, no architecture, no tradeoffs. Sounds like a junior regardless of complexity of what was actually built.
terms.fix: Always frame with business context FIRST. Then architecture. Then technology. Practice explaining your system to someone non-technical first — then add the technical depth layer on top. If you cannot explain the business problem in one sentence, prepare that sentence before the interview.

star.s: Architecture panel: 'Walk me through your current system.' Three senior engineers listening including CTO.
star.t: Demonstrate systems thinking, not just coding skill.
star.a: Drew the patient flow on whiteboard: IMS → Linus Gateway → eCR-Now (GKE) → FHIR query → eCR report → CDC via Direct → IMAP poll → EHR POST. Explained each component's responsibility, why FHIR over HL7 v2, why GKE over bare EC2, how we handle CDC endpoint being slow or down. Mentioned we considered a simpler polling approach but chose event-driven for lower latency.
star.r: CTO said: 'This is exactly the level of architectural thinking we need.' Progressed to offer.

terms.what: Senior engineers communicate differently: they explain WHY before HOW, quantify impact, acknowledge tradeoffs, mention what they considered and rejected, and connect technical decisions to business outcomes.
terms.why: At architect/staff level, you influence decisions across teams. Your ability to communicate risk, tradeoffs, and recommendations clearly determines your actual impact — often more than your coding ability.
terms.how: Key habits: 1) Always state the 'why' before the 'how'. 2) Quantify: 'reduced latency from 3s to 180ms' beats 'improved performance'. 3) Acknowledge tradeoffs: 'We chose X over Y. X gives us A but costs B. We accepted that because...' 4) Mention rejected alternatives: 'We considered polling but chose event-driven because the 5-minute polling lag was too long for CDC 24-hour window.' 5) Connect to business: 'This change reduced CDC report failures, keeping us in compliance and avoiding the $X per-incident penalty.' 6) Invite follow-up: End explanations with 'Any questions on that before I move on?' — shows confidence and controls pace.
terms.where: All stakeholder communication, architecture reviews, incident post-mortems, design documents, 1:1s with management.
terms.when: Always. This mindset shift from 'I built X' to 'I designed X to solve Y because Z, which resulted in W' is what separates senior from staff engineer levels.
terms.issue: Technical people often go too deep too fast — losing non-technical stakeholders in implementation details before establishing why it matters.
terms.fix: Practice the Pyramid Principle: Start with the conclusion/recommendation, then give supporting reasons, then details. Adjust depth based on audience. Read body language — if eyes glaze, zoom out. If they lean forward, go deeper.

star.s: CTO asked why eCR-Now needs to be on GKE vs a simple EC2 instance.
star.t: Justify architectural decision concisely to a non-GKE-expert CTO.
star.a: Said: 'Hospital admission volume spikes 10x during morning rounds. A single EC2 needs to be sized for peak at all times — 90% idle cost. GKE auto-scales pods in 30 seconds based on CPU/memory, so we only pay for what we use. Also, GKE gives us automatic restarts when a pod freezes due to a stuck FHIR connection — critical for our 24-hour CDC reporting SLA.' Three sentences, business-framed.
star.r: CTO immediately approved GKE budget. Technical decision accepted because it was communicated in cost and SLA terms, not Kubernetes terms.

subsections.title: Rate Limiting & API Throttling
subsections.keyRule: Rate Limiting = control how many requests a client can make in a time window. Four algorithms: Token Bucket (bursty allowed, recommended for external APIs), Leaky Bucket (smooth output), Fixed Window (simple, has boundary burst problem), Sliding Window (accurate, higher memory). At 12+ years: know which to use, how to implement distributed rate limiting with Redis, and how to handle the edge cases.

terms.what: Rate limiting controls how many requests a client can make in a time window. Four main algorithms: Token Bucket (bursty traffic allowed up to bucket size), Leaky Bucket (smooth output regardless of burst), Fixed Window Counter (simple per-minute/per-hour counter), Sliding Window Log/Counter (accurate but more memory).
terms.why: Without rate limiting, one abusive or broken client can exhaust all server resources — starving other clients and causing system-wide degradation. Essential for API gateway, FHIR servers, and any public-facing endpoint.
terms.how: Token Bucket: Client has a bucket of N tokens. Each request consumes 1. Tokens refill at rate R per second. Burst up to bucket size is allowed. Best for: external APIs where some burst is acceptable. Implementation: Redis INCR + TTL. Leaky Bucket: Requests enter a fixed-size queue and are processed at a constant rate. Excess requests dropped. Best for: rate-limiting output to downstream (CDC endpoint). Fixed Window: Count requests in a fixed minute/hour window. Reset at boundary. Problem: 100 req at 11:59 + 100 req at 12:00 = 200 in 2 seconds at boundary. Sliding Window Log: Track exact timestamp of each request in a sorted set. Count requests in last N seconds. Accurate. Higher memory (O(requests) per client). Sliding Window Counter: Approximation using two fixed windows. Lower memory. Common choice for production.
terms.where: API Gateway (Spring Cloud Gateway, Kong, AWS API Gateway), FHIR API endpoints, CDC reporting endpoints, any external-facing service.
terms.when: Always at API gateway for external APIs. Per-endpoint for expensive operations (FHIR bulk export, report generation). Service-level for protecting downstream dependencies (CDC, EHR FHIR server).
terms.issue: Fixed Window has a boundary burst problem: double the intended rate passes through at window boundaries. In distributed systems, each instance has its own counter — effective rate becomes N × limit when N instances are running.
terms.fix: Use Redis for distributed rate limiting — single shared counter across all instances. Use Token Bucket (not Fixed Window) for external APIs. Spring Cloud Gateway has built-in Redis RequestRateLimiter filter. For internal service protection, use Resilience4j RateLimiter.

star.s: Interview question today: 'How would you implement rate limiting?' / 'One EHR is sending 1000 req/min to our FHIR API — how do you handle it?'
star.t: Design a distributed, per-client rate limiter that isolates abusive clients without affecting others.
star.a: Token Bucket in Spring Cloud Gateway backed by Redis. Each EHR identified by API key or client certificate CN. Bucket: 100 tokens, refills 100/min. Redis Lua script for atomic check-and-decrement (prevents race condition across instances). Returns HTTP 429 with Retry-After header. Different limits per client tier: certified EHR partners 1000/min, test systems 100/min — configured in Redis hash, hot-updatable without redeployment.
star.r: Isolated abusive client immediately. Other EHR systems unaffected. Abusive client received clear 429 + Retry-After signal to implement proper backoff.

terms.what: Distributed rate limiting enforces a GLOBAL limit across all instances of a service using a shared Redis counter. Without it, 10 instances with a limit of 100/min each = effective limit of 1000/min.
terms.why: Microservices run multiple replicas. Local in-memory rate limiting (Guava RateLimiter) is per-instance — provides no global enforcement. Redis gives a single authoritative counter.
terms.how: Redis Lua Script for atomic sliding window: local key = KEYS[1] local limit = tonumber(ARGV[1]) local window = tonumber(ARGV[2]) local now = tonumber(ARGV[3]) redis.call('ZREMRANGEBYSCORE', key, 0, now - window * 1000) local count = redis.call('ZCARD', key) if count < limit then redis.call('ZADD', key, now, now) redis.call('EXPIRE', key, window) return 1 else return 0 end. Lua script executes atomically — no race condition. Spring Cloud Gateway built-in: spring.cloud.gateway.routes[N].filters: - name: RequestRateLimiter args: redis-rate-limiter.replenishRate: 100 redis-rate-limiter.burstCapacity: 200 key-resolver: '#{@clientIdKeyResolver}'. Cloud-native options: AWS API Gateway, GCP Cloud Endpoints, Kong — all have built-in distributed rate limiting.
terms.where: Any multi-instance service. Critical for: API gateways serving external clients, FHIR servers, payment APIs, any endpoint with per-client SLA.
terms.when: Always use distributed rate limiting if running more than 1 instance. Local limiting gives a false sense of security in distributed deployments — never acceptable in production.
terms.issue: Redis adds ~1-2ms latency per request. Under extreme load (10K+ req/sec), Redis can become a bottleneck. Redis going down makes rate limiting unavailable — fail-open (all requests pass) or fail-closed (all requests blocked)?
terms.fix: Use Redis Cluster for horizontal scaling. For fail-open vs fail-closed: for public APIs use fail-open (availability > perfect rate limiting). For security-sensitive APIs use fail-closed. Add Redis circuit breaker so a slow Redis does not cause service-wide latency. Log and alert on rate limit decisions for abuse detection.

star.s: eCR-Now scaled from 2 to 10 pods. An EHR was sending 800 req/min — expected to be blocked at 100/min but all 10 pods were passing it through independently.
star.t: Fix rate limiting to enforce global limits across all pod replicas.
star.a: Replaced in-memory Guava RateLimiter with Redis-backed distributed sliding window counter. Keyed by EHR client certificate CN. Redis Lua script for atomic counting. 2ms Redis latency overhead — acceptable for this use case. Deployed Redis Sentinel for HA. Configured fail-open: if Redis unavailable, log alert and allow traffic (availability > strict limiting for healthcare systems).
star.r: Global rate limiting enforced correctly regardless of pod count. Single EHR cannot overwhelm system by hitting different pods.

subsections.title: Fault Tolerance & Server Recovery
subsections.keyRule: At 12+ years, you must have rehearsed answers for: 'What happens when X is down?' and 'What if your server gets stuck?' Patterns you must know cold: Circuit Breaker (fail fast when downstream is slow), Retry with exponential backoff + jitter (transient failures), Timeout (never wait forever), Bulkhead (isolate thread pools), Fallback (degrade gracefully). Every external call in production must have timeout + circuit breaker. No exceptions.

terms.term: Server Gets Stuck / Hung — Detection & Recovery
terms.what: A server is 'stuck' when all threads are blocked waiting on a downstream dependency (DB, slow FHIR endpoint, CDC), but the process is still running. It looks healthy from the OS but serves no requests. Classic symptoms: requests timing out, thread pool exhausted, CPU at 0% with no throughput, memory stable.
terms.why: Stuck servers are one of the most common and dangerous production incidents. Load balancers keep routing traffic to stuck instances because the port is open and the process is alive. Without detection, you accumulate slow/failed requests while operators investigate.
terms.how: PREVENTION — Step 1: Set explicit timeouts on ALL external calls. Never use default (usually infinite or 30+ seconds). WebClient: .timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(5)). HikariCP: connection-timeout=3000ms, keepalive-time=30000ms. Kafka consumer: max.poll.interval.ms=300000. Step 2: Thread pool isolation (Bulkhead). Separate thread pool for FHIR calls, CDC calls, DB calls — so one slow dependency does not exhaust the shared pool. Resilience4j Bulkhead: @Bulkhead(name = 'fhirBulkhead', type = Bulkhead.Type.THREADPOOL). Step 3: Circuit Breaker on every external call. DETECTION — Kubernetes liveness probe on /actuator/health with 5s timeout, 3 failure threshold → automatic pod restart. Health check must probe real dependencies (DB query, Redis ping) not just 'return 200'. Custom HealthIndicator: @Component class FhirHealthIndicator implements HealthIndicator { public Health health() { try { fhirClient.ping().timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(2)).block(); return Health.up().build(); } catch(Exception e) { return Health.down().withDetail('fhir', e.getMessage()).build(); } } }. RECOVERY — Kubernetes restarts stuck pods automatically when liveness probe fails. Circuit breaker trips when downstream is slow — stops threads piling up. Thread dump analysis when investigating: jstack <pid> | grep BLOCKED. Look for threads all blocked on same resource.
terms.where: Every production service making synchronous calls to external dependencies. Most dangerous in services calling slow legacy EHRs, government endpoints (CDC Direct), or shared databases.
terms.when: Design for this BEFORE adding features. Set timeouts before you deploy. Add liveness probes before you go live. Every external call needs a timeout — add it when writing the code, not as a hotfix after the incident.
terms.issue: Spring Boot /actuator/health by default only checks if the Spring ApplicationContext loaded — not whether DB, Redis, or FHIR server are actually reachable. A service can be 'UP' while completely unable to process requests.
terms.fix: Implement custom HealthIndicator for each external dependency. Use spring.cloud.gateway built-in health checks. Configure Kubernetes liveness probe with the deep health check endpoint and short enough timeout (5s) and failure threshold (3) to catch stuck pods within 15-20 seconds.

star.s: eCR-Now pods were hanging in production. EHR was upgraded and FHIR responses slowed from 200ms to 35 seconds. All threads blocked waiting on FHIR queries. Service stopped processing new admissions. Kubernetes thought pods were healthy — no liveness probe at the time.
star.t: Recover immediately, prevent recurrence without EHR team involvement.
star.a: Immediate: manually deleted stuck pods — Kubernetes restarted them in 30 seconds. Short-term (same day): Added 5s timeout on all WebClient FHIR calls. Added Resilience4j circuit breaker — after 5 failures, circuit opens for 30s, fail fast. Added liveness probe on /actuator/health/liveness checking FHIR reachability. Long-term: Added Resilience4j Bulkhead — 20 dedicated threads for FHIR, separate from rest of service. Exponential backoff retry: 3 attempts at 1s/2s/4s with jitter.
star.r: Next EHR slow response incident: circuit breaker opened in 10 seconds, liveness probe restarted pod in 45 seconds total. Reports queued in Kafka retry topic. Zero missed CDC reports. MTTR dropped from 45 minutes (manual) to 45 seconds (automated).

terms.what: Circuit Breaker wraps calls to external services and tracks failure rate. CLOSED: calls pass through normally. After failure threshold: OPEN — calls fail immediately without hitting downstream, freeing threads. After timeout: HALF-OPEN — test call. Success → CLOSED. Failure → OPEN again.
terms.why: Without circuit breaker: slow downstream causes thread pool exhaustion → entire service stops responding. Circuit breaker fails fast, freeing threads immediately, allowing the service to handle other requests.
terms.how: @CircuitBreaker(name = 'fhirService', fallbackMethod = 'fhirFallback') public Mono<Bundle> queryFhir(String patientId) { return fhirClient.get(...); } public Mono<Bundle> fhirFallback(String patientId, CallNotPermittedException ex) { return Mono.just(Bundle.empty()); }. application.yml: resilience4j: circuitbreaker: instances: fhirService: sliding-window-type: COUNT_BASED sliding-window-size: 10 failure-rate-threshold: 50 slow-call-duration-threshold: 3s slow-call-rate-threshold: 50 wait-duration-in-open-state: 30s permitted-number-of-calls-in-half-open-state: 3 minimum-number-of-calls: 5. Key: minimum-number-of-calls prevents circuit opening on first few calls at startup.
terms.where: Every synchronous call to an external service: FHIR API, CDC Direct endpoint, database in high-throughput path, any third-party API. Never skip this in production.
terms.when: Add circuit breaker when writing the integration code, not as a reactive hotfix. Rule of thumb: if the call crosses a network boundary, it needs a circuit breaker and a timeout.
terms.issue: Misconfigured thresholds cause two problems: 1) Too sensitive (10% threshold) — circuit opens on normal transient errors, causing cascading failures. 2) Too insensitive (90% threshold) — circuit opens too late, after many threads already blocked.
terms.fix: Start with: failure-rate-threshold: 50, minimum-number-of-calls: 10, sliding-window-size: 20. Tune based on observed error rates in production. Add slow call rate threshold separately (slow calls are as dangerous as errors). Test with Chaos Engineering: kill downstream, verify circuit opens and fallback works within expected time.

star.s: CDC Direct messaging endpoint was intermittently slow (government infrastructure maintenance). eCR-Now threads piled up during CDC slowness → service degraded, missing 24-hour reporting window.
star.t: Isolate CDC slowness from rest of eCR-Now service. Ensure reports eventually reach CDC even if endpoint is slow.
star.a: Added Resilience4j circuit breaker on CDC Direct send. Fallback: enqueue report to a Kafka retry topic instead of dropping it. Circuit: 50% failure rate in 10-call window → OPEN for 30s. Kafka retry consumer retries with exponential backoff: 1min, 5min, 15min, 1hr, 24hr. Alert when circuit stays OPEN >5 minutes (PagerDuty). Dashboards showing open/closed state per dependency.
star.r: CDC slowness no longer affects eCR-Now service. Reports queued and delivered when CDC recovers. Zero missed reportable condition reports. Team gets alerted before SLA breach.

terms.what: Retry: automatically retry failed operations on transient errors with exponential backoff + jitter. Timeout: stop waiting after configured duration. Together, they are the foundation of resilient distributed systems.
terms.why: Network glitches, brief service restarts, GC pauses cause transient failures. A single retry resolves ~80% of transient failures. Without timeout, threads wait forever — this is how services get stuck.
terms.how: Resilience4j Retry: @Retry(name = 'fhirQuery', fallbackMethod = 'fallback'). Config: max-attempts: 3, wait-duration: 1s, exponential-backoff-multiplier: 2.0, randomized-wait-factor: 0.3 (jitter), retry-exceptions: [IOException, TimeoutException, WebClientRequestException]. Do NOT include: HttpClientErrorException (4xx = caller error, not transient). WebClient timeout: webClient.get().uri(url).retrieve().bodyToMono(Patient.class).timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(5)). HikariCP: connectionTimeout=3000, validationTimeout=2000, maxLifetime=1800000. Always: timeout < retry-wait. Otherwise previous attempt still running when retry starts. Standard timeouts: internal services 1-3s, FHIR EHR server 5s, CDC Direct 15-30s (government infra is slow), DB 3s.
terms.where: All HTTP client calls (WebClient, RestTemplate, Feign), DB calls in high-throughput paths, Kafka consumer processing, external API integrations.
terms.when: ONLY retry idempotent operations. GET = safe to retry. POST = only retry if server is idempotent (uses idempotency key). NEVER retry without backoff + jitter — causes thundering herd when all instances retry simultaneously and overwhelm a recovering service.
terms.issue: Retrying non-idempotent operations (CDC report submission) causes duplicate reports. Retrying without jitter causes thundering herd — all pods retry at exact same interval, spike of retries overwhelms recovering service.
terms.fix: Check idempotency before enabling retry. Use idempotency key (encounterID + timestamp hash) for POST operations. Always add randomized-wait-factor (jitter) — spreads retries across the retry window. Cap maximum attempts at 3-5. Add retry budget — if >20% of calls are retrying, alert and stop retrying.

star.s: FHIR patient queries intermittently failing due to EHR GC pauses (500ms GC stops causing connection reset). No retry = occasional missed condition detection.
star.t: Add resilient retry without overloading the EHR during its GC recoveries.
star.a: Resilience4j Retry: 3 attempts, exponential backoff 1s/2s/4s with 30% jitter. Only retry on IOException, TimeoutException — not 400/404 (those are data errors, not transient). FHIR queries are GET (idempotent) so retry is safe. Added 5s timeout per call. Metric: retry-attempt counter in Prometheus — alerting if >5% of calls hit retry.
star.r: GC-pause failures resolved transparently. EHR not overwhelmed by simultaneous retries. Condition detection rate: 99.9%.

subsections.title: Must-Know Patterns for Senior/Staff Role
subsections.keyRule: At 12+ years you must know when NOT to use patterns, not just how they work. Saga: only for multi-service workflows spanning multiple DBs. Outbox: whenever you need both DB write and event publish to be atomic. CQRS: only when read and write models genuinely diverge. Idempotency: every POST that might be retried. Event Sourcing: only when full audit trail is a business requirement.

terms.what: Saga is a sequence of local transactions where each step publishes an event on success, and compensating transactions undo previous steps on failure. Replaces XA/2PC distributed transactions across microservices.
terms.why: In microservices, each service has its own database. You cannot use a database transaction across service boundaries. Saga provides eventual consistency without distributed locks or XA transactions (which are slow and create coupling).
terms.how: Two flavors: Choreography Saga (event-driven, decentralized): Patient Admitted → eCR-Now subscribes → queries FHIR → publishes ECRGenerated → CDC service subscribes → sends to CDC → publishes ECRSubmitted. On failure: service publishes ECRFailed → compensating actions triggered (alert operations team, queue for manual review). Orchestration Saga (centralized): A Saga Orchestrator (state machine) tells each service what to do next. More control, easier to monitor, easier to debug. Use Spring State Machine or Temporal for complex healthcare workflows.
terms.where: Multi-service workflows spanning multiple databases: healthcare patient admission flow, e-commerce order flow (payment, inventory, shipping), financial transaction processing.
terms.when: Use Saga when a business process spans 3+ services each with their own DB. Do NOT use for 2-service interactions — simple event publishing is enough. Do NOT use when strict ACID consistency is needed (use a shared database or accept the architecture limitation).
terms.issue: Compensating transactions can fail too. A saga stuck in a partial state (3 of 5 steps done, compensation of step 2 failed) is very hard to recover and requires manual intervention.
terms.fix: All compensating transactions must be idempotent. Persist saga state in DB — can replay or manually advance. Dead-letter queue for failed compensations. Monitoring dashboard showing saga state per patient/order. Define a clear manual escalation process for stuck sagas.

star.s: eCR-Now patient flow has 5 steps: launchPatient → FHIR query → reportability check → eCR generation → CDC submission. Any step can fail. Need reliable end-to-end delivery with no missed CDC reports.
star.t: Design reliable multi-step workflow without XA distributed transaction.
star.a: Choreography Saga with Kafka. Each step publishes success/failure event to its own topic. Failed steps publish to a per-step retry topic with exponential backoff. Saga state (patientId, encounterId, currentStep, timestamp) persisted in eCR-Now DB. Alert when saga not completed within 4 hours of patient admission (well within CDC 24h window).
star.r: Full audit trail per patient encounter. Automatic retry on transient failures. SLA breach alerts give operations team time to intervene. Zero missed CDC reports in 6 months post-implementation.

terms.what: Outbox Pattern ensures a DB write and event publish happen atomically — solving the dual-write problem. Write the event to an OUTBOX table in the same DB transaction as the business data. A separate process reads the outbox table and publishes to Kafka/RabbitMQ, then marks as published.
terms.why: Without outbox: save to DB succeeds, then publish to Kafka fails → data in DB but event lost → downstream services never get notified → inconsistency. Or publish succeeds, DB fails → event without data in DB. The outbox pattern makes this atomic.
terms.how: Step 1: In same DB transaction: INSERT INTO ecr_reports (encounter_id, data) and INSERT INTO outbox (aggregate_type, aggregate_id, event_type, payload). Step 2: Outbox Poller (scheduled every 1s or CDC-based): SELECT * FROM outbox WHERE published = false ORDER BY created_at LIMIT 100. For each row: publish to Kafka. On success: UPDATE outbox SET published = true. On failure: leave for next poll (natural retry). Debezium (Change Data Capture): watch DB binlog, stream INSERT events from outbox table to Kafka — more efficient than polling, near real-time.
terms.where: Any time you need to both update a DB and publish an event — patient admitted event, eCR report generated event, CDC submission event. Critical when downstream system availability is uncertain.
terms.when: Use outbox whenever event publishing to Kafka must be guaranteed. Skip it for fire-and-forget notifications where loss is acceptable. Use Debezium when you need near real-time (polling has 1-5s delay).
terms.issue: Outbox table grows indefinitely if cleanup is not implemented. Old published entries accumulate, causing full table scans to become slow.
terms.fix: Scheduled cleanup: DELETE FROM outbox WHERE published = true AND created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL 7 DAY. Run in batches, not a single DELETE of all rows. Add index on (published, created_at).

star.s: eCR-Now was losing patient admission events when Kafka was briefly unavailable during broker rebalancing. The patient FHIR query step was missed, causing delayed CDC reports.
star.t: Guarantee event delivery for every patient admission without Kafka-coupled DB writes.
star.a: Implemented Outbox pattern: patient admission data written to ecr_reports table and outbox table in same transaction. Debezium watches outbox table via MySQL binlog, streams to Kafka. If Kafka is down, Debezium buffers and delivers when Kafka recovers. Outbox table has 7-day retention with daily cleanup batch.
star.r: Zero lost patient admission events. Even during Kafka rolling upgrade, all admissions processed in correct order. Decoupled FHIR query trigger from Kafka availability.

terms.what: An operation is idempotent if calling it multiple times produces the same result as calling once. Critical for safe retries. GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, PUT, DELETE are naturally idempotent. POST needs explicit idempotency key design.
terms.why: Networks fail. Retries are necessary. Without idempotency: retries cause duplicate orders, duplicate payments, duplicate CDC reports. In healthcare, duplicate CDC reports is a compliance violation and wastes CDC reviewer time.
terms.how: Client generates UUID per request and sends in Idempotency-Key header (or in POST body). Server: 1) Check Redis/DB for key. 2) If found: return cached response (no reprocessing). 3) If not found: process, store key + result in Redis with TTL (24h typical). 4) Return result. DB safety net: unique constraint on idempotency_key column — catches race condition between two concurrent identical requests. Redis: hash map idempotency_key → response JSON. Key: always store the RESPONSE not just 'processed=true' so you return identical response on retry.
terms.where: All state-changing POST operations that might be retried: CDC report submission, EHR document creation, payment processing, Kafka consumer (use message offset/ID as idempotency key).
terms.when: Design for idempotency when writing the API, not retroactively. Rule: if the operation has side effects and might be retried, it needs idempotency. Pure reads (GET) are inherently idempotent — no extra work needed.
terms.issue: Idempotency key stored only in Redis can be lost if Redis crashes before expiry. A retry after Redis restart would reprocess the request — duplicate CDC submission.
terms.fix: Persist idempotency key in database (indexed column) as source of truth. Use Redis as cache (fast path). If Redis miss: fall back to DB check. On DB hit: re-cache in Redis. TTL must be long enough to cover client retry window (clients should retry within 24h, set TTL to 48h).

star.s: CDC submission service was timing out intermittently. Retry logic was retrying the POST to CDC. CDC received same eCR report 2-3 times. Compliance team flagged duplicate reports as a protocol violation.
star.t: Prevent duplicate CDC submissions when retry logic fires.
star.a: Idempotency key = SHA256(encounterId + reportGeneratedAt). Stored in eCR-Now DB with unique constraint. Before POST to CDC: check if key exists. If yes: return previous response. CDC also provides a tracking ID in response — stored and deduplicated on their processing side too. Added Idempotency-Key header to CDC request (their API supports it).
star.r: Zero duplicate CDC reports. Retry logic safe to run aggressively. Compliance team confirmed protocol compliance.

subsections.title: Performance & Scalability at Scale
subsections.keyRule: At 12+ years, performance is measurement-first, optimization-second. Know your numbers: p50/p95/p99 latency, throughput (req/sec), error rate. Profile before optimizing. The bottleneck is almost always NOT what you think it is. Top bottlenecks in order of frequency: 1) N+1 DB queries, 2) Missing DB indexes, 3) Unoptimized external API calls, 4) Unnecessary serialization, 5) Thread contention.

terms.what: Systematic performance improvement: Establish baseline → Profile to find bottleneck → Form hypothesis → Change ONE variable → Measure → Document. Never optimize without a benchmark. Never change multiple things simultaneously.
terms.why: Random optimization wastes weeks on code paths that are not in the hot path. Senior engineers are expected to bring data to performance discussions, not intuition. Business stakeholders need projected impact before approving optimization work.
terms.how: Step 1 — Baseline: Run load test (Gatling/JMeter), capture p50/p95/p99 latency, throughput, CPU/Memory, error rate. Step 2 — Identify bottleneck: CPU-bound? (async-profiler, Java Flight Recorder). Memory/GC-bound? (heap dump in MAT or VisualVM, GC logs). I/O-bound? (slow DB queries via EXPLAIN plan, N+1 via Hibernate SQL logging, external API latency). Thread contention? (jstack, lock profiling in JFR). Step 3 — Fix one thing: add DB index, fix N+1, add caching, increase pool size, switch to async. Step 4 — Measure: run same load test, compare delta. Step 5 — Document: bottleneck found, fix applied, improvement measured. For team knowledge and future reference.
terms.where: User-facing APIs (<100ms p95 target), critical business flows (eCR processing < 2min), batch jobs (throughput target per hour).
terms.when: Performance tuning is reactive when SLA is breached, proactive via load testing before major releases. Quarterly performance review on critical paths for senior engineers.
terms.issue: Premature optimization: spending weeks on code that is not on the hot path. Also: optimizing without load testing means you fix local benchmarks that don't reflect real production traffic patterns.
terms.fix: Always profile in production-like environment with production-like data volumes. Synthetic data in staging with 100 rows vs production with 10 million rows = completely different bottlenecks. Use flame graphs from async-profiler to visualize where CPU time actually goes.

star.s: eCR-Now FHIR patient query taking 3 seconds p95. Missing CDC 24-hour reporting window for late-admitted patients.
star.t: Reduce FHIR query latency to under 500ms p95 without changing the EHR FHIR server.
star.a: Profiled with async-profiler: 80% of time in Jackson deserialization of 50KB FHIR Bundle responses. Fix 1: Added FHIR _elements parameter to request only needed fields → response shrunk from 50KB to 6KB → deserialization from 400ms to 50ms. Fix 2: Redis cache for FHIR patient data — 5-minute TTL (most queries within 5-min admission window). Cache hit rate: 70%. Fix 3: Parallel FHIR queries for Condition, Observation, MedicationRequest using CompletableFuture.allOf() instead of sequential. Total improvement: 3s sequential → 180ms parallel with caching.
star.r: FHIR query latency: 3000ms → 180ms p95. 100% CDC 24-hour compliance restored. Cache reduced EHR FHIR server load by 70%.

terms.what: GKE Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) scales pod replicas based on CPU/memory/custom metrics. Cluster Autoscaler scales the underlying node pool. Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) adjusts pod resource requests/limits automatically.
terms.why: Hospital admission volume is bursty — 10x higher during morning rounds (7-9am) than midnight. Static sizing wastes money at off-peak and underserves at peak. Autoscaling handles this automatically.
terms.how: HPA: kubectl autoscale deployment ecr-now --cpu-percent=70 --min=2 --max=20. Or YAML: spec.metrics: type: Resource, resource.name: cpu, target.averageUtilization: 70. Custom metrics (e.g., Kafka lag): HPA with KEDA (Kubernetes Event-driven Autoscaling). KEDA scales pods based on Kafka consumer group lag — ideal for eCR-Now. Pod Disruption Budget (PDB): ensures minimum number of pods always available during node drains or updates. Liveness Probe: restarts stuck pods. Readiness Probe: removes pod from load balancer until ready (prevents traffic to starting pod). Resource Requests/Limits: requests.cpu: 500m, requests.memory: 512Mi, limits.cpu: 2000m, limits.memory: 2Gi. Requests used for scheduling, limits for enforcement.
terms.where: Any GKE/Kubernetes deployment handling variable load. eCR-Now: scales with admission volume. Healthcare systems: scale with shift changes and clinic hours.
terms.when: Configure HPA from day one. Do not manually scale — it is error-prone and slow. KEDA is preferred over CPU-based HPA for queue-driven workloads because CPU lags behind queue depth.
terms.issue: HPA scaling on CPU has lag — CPU spikes after load arrives, not before. By the time pods scale up (30-60 seconds), burst is already failing. Pods start up slowly (JVM warm-up, FHIR client initialization) — 60+ seconds to first request served.
terms.fix: Predictive scaling: schedule extra replicas before known peak times (morning rounds). KEDA on Kafka lag for queue-driven services — starts scaling as queue grows, before CPU spikes. Spring Boot AOT compilation + CRaC (Checkpoint/Restore) for sub-second pod startup. Readiness probe ensures traffic only routes to warmed-up pods.

star.s: Morning rounds caused 10x admission spike. eCR-Now CPU HPA was too slow — by the time pods scaled up (60 seconds), admission queue had backed up. Reports delayed by 15 minutes.
star.t: Eliminate admission burst impact on eCR report latency.
star.a: Switched from CPU-based HPA to KEDA scaled on Kafka consumer group lag. Threshold: scale up when lag > 50 messages. Scale down when lag < 10 for 5 minutes. Added pre-scheduled scale: CronJob scales to 10 pods at 6:30am, back to 2 at 10am. Added /actuator/health/readiness probe checking FHIR client warm-up before accepting traffic.
star.r: Morning burst handled with pre-provisioned pods. Queue lag stays < 10 messages even at peak. eCR reports generated within 2 minutes of admission across all load levels.

subsections.title: How to Stay Sharp & Keep Growing at 12+ Years
subsections.keyRule: At 12+ years, growth is no longer about learning new frameworks. It is about depth, breadth of tradeoffs, system thinking, communication, and influence. The most impactful areas to invest: cloud-native patterns (GKE, observability), reactive/async programming, architecture documentation, and technical leadership skills.

terms.what: Structured approach to staying technically sharp while growing leadership influence. Focus on three layers: depth (go deeper in your core stack), breadth (understand adjacent technologies), and visibility (document and share what you know).
terms.why: Technology changes fast. A 12-year engineer who stopped learning at year 8 is effectively a 4-year engineer with 12 years of salary expectation. Continuous learning is non-negotiable for staying relevant and promotable.
terms.how: Weekly habits: 1) Read one architecture blog per week (Martin Fowler, AWS Architecture Blog, Google Cloud Blog, InfoQ). 2) One technical book per quarter (Designing Data-Intensive Applications, Building Microservices, Clean Architecture). 3) 30 minutes daily on new tech (hands-on, not just reading). Monthly: contribute one ADR (Architecture Decision Record) for a team decision. Quarterly: deliver one tech talk or brown bag session. Yearly: get one certification (AWS/GCP Solutions Architect, CKA for Kubernetes, Spring Professional). Specific areas to focus for 2025: Observability (distributed tracing, OpenTelemetry), AI/LLM integration in healthcare (FHIR + AI for condition detection), cloud cost optimization (FinOps), and data streaming (Kafka → Apache Flink).
terms.where: Personal learning is most effective in small daily habits. Apply new learning immediately on real work — not toy projects.
terms.when: Consistent > intense. 30 minutes daily is better than 8-hour weekend sprints. Schedule it like a meeting — it disappears without scheduling.
terms.issue: Tutorial hell: watching/reading about technologies without hands-on practice. The illusion of learning without actual skill development.
terms.fix: Build something with every new technology you learn. For rate limiting: implement a Redis rate limiter from scratch. For Kubernetes: deploy your own service on GKE with HPA and liveness probes. The act of building surfaces all the gaps that tutorials miss.

star.s: Job market became more competitive. Java 8 skills alone no longer sufficient for senior positions. Colleagues with cloud and architecture skills getting promoted.
star.t: Modernize skill set while maintaining delivery on current project.
star.a: Created a personal learning roadmap: Q1: GKE + Kubernetes CKA certification. Q2: Reactive programming (Spring WebFlux, Project Reactor). Q3: OpenTelemetry + distributed tracing in eCR-Now. Q4: Kafka Streams for real-time condition monitoring. Applied each skill to real eCR-Now features within 2 weeks of learning.
star.r: Migrated eCR-Now to reactive WebClient (20% throughput improvement). Added distributed tracing (reduced incident investigation from 2 hours to 15 minutes). Passed CKA. Promoted to senior architect role.

terms.term: Excelling in Architecture Meetings & Tech Discussions
terms.what: Strategies for contributing effectively in architecture reviews, design sessions, and technical leadership meetings — where impact comes from clear thinking and communication, not just deep technical knowledge.
terms.why: At 12+ years, your influence is through others. How you communicate in architecture meetings determines whether your ideas get adopted, whether you are seen as a leader, and whether you get invited to higher-level discussions.
terms.how: Before the meeting: read any design docs or tickets. Identify the core tradeoffs. Prepare 1-2 clarifying questions and 1-2 concrete suggestions. In the meeting: 1) Listen first, understand the problem fully before proposing solutions. 2) When you speak: state assumption, then reasoning, then recommendation. 'My assumption is we need <99% availability. Given that, I'd recommend X over Y because...' 3) Quantify when possible: 'X adds 2ms latency per request — at 1000 req/sec that's 2s of cumulative overhead, is that acceptable?' 4) Acknowledge tradeoffs: 'X is simpler but Y is more scalable — what's our 3-year scale projection?' 5) When you disagree: 'I see the appeal of X. My concern is Y. Can we discuss how to mitigate that?' After the meeting: write the decision as an ADR (Architecture Decision Record). This positions you as the person who drives clarity and documentation.
terms.where: Architecture reviews, design sessions, incident post-mortems, technology evaluations, vendor discussions.
terms.when: Every time you are in a room with architects, staff engineers, or technical leadership. These are your opportunities to demonstrate seniority beyond your individual code contributions.
terms.issue: Experienced engineers often speak too technically too soon — alienating non-technical stakeholders and losing the room before making their point.
terms.fix: Match your communication depth to the audience. With engineers: full technical depth. With product managers: business impact and risk. With executives: cost, time, and risk in one sentence. Prepare audience-appropriate versions of your technical points.

star.s: Architecture review: debate about whether eCR-Now should use synchronous FHIR queries vs async event-driven. Two senior engineers on opposite sides. Meeting at risk of going in circles.
star.t: Facilitate to a clear recommendation and decision.
star.a: Drew a decision matrix on the whiteboard: criteria (latency, reliability, CDC compliance, implementation complexity) rated 1-5 for each option. Added quantitative estimates where available: sync adds 200ms latency but simplifies error handling. Async reduces latency but adds Kafka operational complexity. Named the key constraint: CDC 24-hour window gives us slack — eventual consistency is acceptable. Recommended: async for primary flow, sync as fallback for urgent cases. Proposed to write ADR to document decision and revisit in 6 months.
star.r: Meeting ended with clear decision. ADR written and approved. Both engineers aligned. Decision referenced 6 months later when adding a new EHR integration — saved 2 weeks of re-debate.
